title: How to Win the Defense Innovation Contest
gist: Former Pentagon policy chief Kahl argues no single country—not even the U.S.—can produce drones, ships, and munitions at the scale needed for protracted war, so America and trusted allies must pool private-sector capital, comparative advantages, and distributed production while channeling (not defeating) defense protectionism.

themes.theme: The case for pooling over silos

insights.claim: No single country, not even the United States, can by itself produce drones, ships, and critical munitions at sufficient scale or maintain a defense industrial base adaptable enough to prevail in a protracted high-intensity conflict.
insights.speaker: Colin H. Kahl
insights.so_what: Undercuts the domestic-only defense buildup thesis and points to transnational coproduction as the winning model.
insights.locator: Washington’s alliances are under immense strain

insights.claim: The war with Iran rapidly depleted U.S. stockpiles of high-end long-range strike munitions and air-defense interceptors, while U.S. bases, Gulf infrastructure, and Strait of Hormuz shipping proved vulnerable to cheap and plentiful Iranian missiles and drones.
insights.speaker: Colin H. Kahl
insights.so_what: Real-world proof that legacy platforms plus thin magazines lose to cheap mass—driving demand for scaled, attritable production.
insights.locator: Washington’s alliances are under immense strain

insights.claim: U.S. adversaries are already deepening defense-industrial cooperation: Iran shared drone technology with Russia, North Korea provided munitions and troops, and China supplied Russia dual-use components and economic relief in exchange for advanced Russian weapons and intelligence.
insights.speaker: Colin H. Kahl
insights.so_what: The adversary bloc's pooling raises the urgency for allied coproduction to keep a technological edge.
insights.locator: This is especially true at a time

insights.claim: Traditional government-to-government defense cooperation—foreign military sales, bilateral coproduction, NATO procurement—is too slow for a battlefield where technology changes in weeks not years, so the real momentum lies among private firms and research institutions.
insights.speaker: Colin H. Kahl
insights.so_what: Points investors toward agile private defense-tech firms and cross-border partnerships over legacy prime-contractor channels.

themes.theme: Comparative advantage and division of labor

insights.claim: Allied comparative advantages are concentrated: Switzerland has the world's highest density of AI talent, Japan and South Korea have shipbuilding and semiconductor fab capacity exceeding current U.S. output, Germany excels at precision manufacturing, Brazil in rare earths and aerospace, India in software and low-cost scale, and Israel in cyber and missile defense.
insights.speaker: Colin H. Kahl
insights.so_what: A map of where allied defense-tech value pools—useful for identifying cross-border partnership and investment targets.

insights.claim: Cross-border defense partnerships are proliferating: Saab and General Atomics on an unmanned early-warning system, Anduril and Germany's Rheinmetall on autonomous counterdrone systems for Europe, South Korea's Hanwha with Rhode Island's Vatn Systems on undersea drones for the U.S. Navy, and Auterion's Artemis deep-strike drone built across Ukraine, Germany, and the U.S.
insights.speaker: Colin H. Kahl
insights.so_what: Concrete named partnerships flag the emerging transnational defense-tech ecosystem to track.

insights.claim: The U.S. is securing rare-earth supply through allies: the Development Finance Corporation approved a $565 million loan to Brazil's Serra Verde (with an option to acquire a minority equity stake) and Australia's Lynas Rare Earths won a U.S. contract to build a processing facility in Texas, reducing dependence on China-dominated supply.
insights.speaker: Colin H. Kahl
insights.so_what: Direct signal of U.S. government capital flowing to friendly rare-earth producers—an investable de-risking-from-China theme.
insights.locator: Certain countries offer advantages based on their place

insights.claim: Modern strategy relies on 'precise, attritable mass'—cheap replaceable drones and long-range munitions produced near war theaters to cut costs and avoid transport an adversary could disrupt, as with Helsing's European 'resilience factories' and Anduril's Australian-made Ghost Shark undersea vehicle.
insights.speaker: Colin H. Kahl
insights.so_what: Distributed, theater-adjacent production is the emerging manufacturing model—favoring firms building resilient forward factories.

insights.claim: Coproduction under U.S. license already replenishes depleted stocks: Japan's Mitsubishi Heavy Industries makes Patriot missiles under RTX and Lockheed Martin license, and Northrop Grumman with Poland's Niewiadow-PGM agreed to jointly produce more than 180,000 155mm artillery shells annually.
insights.speaker: Colin H. Kahl
insights.so_what: Shows licensed allied coproduction is a proven mechanism to rebuild magazines depleted by Ukraine and Iran.

insights.claim: The best AI chips are designed by U.S. firms but mostly fabricated in Taiwan and require extreme ultraviolet lithography systems built exclusively by the Dutch company ASML, illustrating that even cutting-edge U.S. capability depends on allied inputs.
insights.speaker: Colin H. Kahl
insights.so_what: Reinforces the structural interdependence of the defense-tech supply chain—no bloc is self-sufficient.

insights.claim: Defense markets suffer from monopsony—a few large incumbent firms facing a single government buyer—which guarantees demand and gives little incentive to control costs, move quickly, or innovate, and cross-border scale could entrench giants and lock out small players absent deliberate policy.
insights.speaker: Colin H. Kahl
insights.so_what: Whether small innovators can enter transnational arrangements is a policy choice that will shape defense-tech competitive dynamics.
insights.locator: More firms bidding for defense contracts

insights.claim: As European countries pursue national rearmament they risk building complex systems not interoperable with each other or with U.S. platforms, ceding the logistics-and-maintenance advantage that shared platforms like the Lockheed Martin F-35 provide.
insights.speaker: Colin H. Kahl
insights.so_what: Interoperability fragmentation is a hidden cost of Europe's rearmament that favors firms adopting common standards early.

insights.claim: Defense protectionism is hardening into law: the U.S. is raising Buy American requirements while the EU's $175 billion Security Action for Europe instrument funds only projects where no more than 35 percent of components are external, and the European Defence Industry Programme requires 65 percent EU, Norway, or Ukraine content.
insights.speaker: Colin H. Kahl
insights.so_what: Explicit content thresholds define which suppliers can access hundreds of billions in Western defense spending.
insights.locator: These instincts are hardening into law

insights.claim: Kahl argues policymakers should channel rather than defeat economic nationalism, because protectionist rules govern finished platforms and prime contracts, leaving room for the lower-visibility web of component supply, software, talent transfer, and joint development that drives modern innovation.
insights.speaker: Colin H. Kahl
insights.so_what: The workaround—dual-use components and subsystems under prime-contract carve-outs—is where cross-border defense investment can still flow.
insights.locator: Policies that enable transnational defense innovation

insights.claim: The 2026 National Defense Authorization Act requires creating an assistant secretary of defense for international armaments cooperation, which Kahl says needs real authority over export-licensing coordination to foster a common allied defense innovation ecosystem.
insights.speaker: Colin H. Kahl
insights.so_what: A new institutional post that could ease export controls and accelerate allied coproduction if resourced.
insights.locator: Some technology exchanges and joint innovation efforts
